concept: Equivalent / Matching in a Stated Meaning, Function, or Effect
description: Matching another thing in the meaning, function, or effect selected for comparison while differing in form. Two formats may be equivalent for one task and diverge where timing, detail, or interaction changes; 'senao' (same) claims identity or broad sameness, while 'samethu' names a bounded match.
sound_symbolism: Steady 'sa' and centered 'me' hold two forms at the same point of comparison. Across their remaining difference, 'thu' breathes instead of closing them into one object.
grammatical_notes: A predicative or attributive adjective, often with a plural subject: 'lo phelotu samethu nai.' (The formats are equivalent.) A comparison clause can name the selected meaning, function, evidence, and limits. The quality-noun rule supplies equivalence.

pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: Different forms can carry the same chosen relation without becoming visually, materially, or sensorially identical.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A bounded equivalence claim can be checked and corrected when a paraphrase, caption, or interpretation loses what one participant needed.

tags.cognition: a selected match in meaning or function
tags.dialogue: comparison between different expressions or formats
